data_flow_trace.original: GOVERNANCE calls GovernanceConfig.setForNextEpoch(threshold, stake, type(uint64).max) — _validateConfig only checks votingDurationMicros > 0; type(uint64).max passes
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: _validateConfig now enforces MIN_VOTING_DURATION (1 hour * 1e6 us) <= _votingDurationMicros <= MAX_VOTING_DURATION (365 days * 1e6 us ≈ 3.1536e13). A call with type(uint64).max (~1.8447e19) reverts with VotingDurationTooLong at line 210-212. The ancillary guards on the other two fields (ExcessiveMinVotingThreshold at line 197-199 and ExcessiveRequiredProposerStake at line 204-206) have also been added.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: setForNextEpoch re-invokes _validateConfig at line 139 before storing pending config; no path bypasses this validation on live config writes (initialize at line 92 also validates).
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: The issue premise is obsolete: upper bound on votingDurationMicros is now hard-capped at 365 days.

data_flow_trace.original: At epoch boundary, RECONFIGURATION.applyPendingConfig() writes type(uint64).max to live storage
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: applyPendingConfig copies _pendingConfig to live fields at lines 167-169, but the pending config itself can never hold a value above MAX_VOTING_DURATION because setForNextEpoch validated it. Step is not reachable with the adversarial value.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: Only RECONFIGURATION role can call; runs only at epoch boundary and only if hasPendingConfig is true.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Pending slot cannot contain type(uint64).max due to upstream validation.

data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: createProposal still reads now_ = _now() at line 300, votingDuration = _config().votingDurationMicros() at line 301, and computes expirationTime = now_ + votingDuration at line 302 in unchecked-free Solidity 0.8.30 arithmetic. The arithmetic itself is unchanged, but votingDuration is now bounded to <= 365 days * 1e6 us.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: MAX_VOTING_DURATION (~3.1536e13) plus any reasonable unix-micro now_ is far below uint64.max (~1.8447e19); overflow is mathematically impossible for the next ~584,000 years.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: Attack precondition (voting duration near uint64.max) cannot be met.

data_flow_trace.original: uint64 expirationTime = now_ + votingDuration overflows because now_ + type(uint64).max > type(uint64).max
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: With votingDuration capped at 365 days * 1e6 us, now_ + votingDuration cannot reach 2^64 for realistic timestamps; no overflow revert.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: Bounded by enforced MAX_VOTING_DURATION at GovernanceConfig.sol:210-212.

data_flow_trace.original: All createProposal() calls revert permanently — permanent governance DoS
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: createProposal remains callable; no state exists in which votingDurationMicros can be set high enough to trigger arithmetic overflow on expirationTime.
data_flow_trace.differs_from_original: DoS scenario cannot be triggered by setting voting duration to uint64.max.

reasoning_chain: The issue's central premise is that _validateConfig only rejects votingDurationMicros == 0, allowing a malicious proposal to set it to type(uint64).max, thereby overflowing now_ + votingDuration inside createProposal() and freezing governance. In the current code (src/runtime/GovernanceConfig.sol:188-213), _validateConfig enforces both a lower bound (MIN_VOTING_DURATION = 1 hour in micros, line 207-209) and an upper bound (MAX_VOTING_DURATION = 365 days in micros ≈ 3.1536e13, line 210-212). Any attempt to call setForNextEpoch(…, type(uint64).max) reverts with VotingDurationTooLong and never reaches the pending storage slot, so applyPendingConfig cannot propagate such a value to live storage. Consequently, createProposal at src/governance/Governance.sol:300-302 always reads a votingDuration <= 365 days in microseconds, and now_ + votingDuration stays orders of magnitude below 2^64. The same hardening is applied symmetrically to minVotingThreshold (ExcessiveMinVotingThreshold, line 197-199) and requiredProposerStake (ExcessiveRequiredProposerStake, line 204-206), indicating a deliberate bounds-hardening pass. The issue is fixed at the validator, not merely mitigated downstream.

key_evidence.excerpt:
uint64 public constant MIN_VOTING_DURATION = uint64(1 hours) * 1_000_000;
uint64 public constant MAX_VOTING_DURATION = uint64(365 days) * 1_000_000;
key_evidence.why_matters: Introduces the upper bound constant that did not exist in the original issue's premise.

key_evidence.excerpt:
if (_votingDurationMicros < MIN_VOTING_DURATION) { revert Errors.VotingDurationTooShort(_votingDurationMicros, MIN_VOTING_DURATION); }
if (_votingDurationMicros > MAX_VOTING_DURATION) { revert Errors.VotingDurationTooLong(_votingDurationMicros, MAX_VOTING_DURATION); }
key_evidence.why_matters: Directly blocks setForNextEpoch / initialize from ever storing a votingDurationMicros > 365 days in micros, which is the precondition for the claimed uint64 overflow.

key_evidence.excerpt: function setForNextEpoch(...) external { requireAllowed(SystemAddresses.GOVERNANCE); _requireInitialized(); _validateConfig(_minVotingThreshold, _requiredProposerStake, _votingDurationMicros); _pendingConfig = PendingConfig({...}); hasPendingConfig = true; ... }
key_evidence.why_matters: Confirms _validateConfig is invoked on every governance-initiated pending config update — there is no bypass path.
The addition the issue pointed to; unchanged, but now safe because the input votingDuration is bounded to <= 365 days * 1e6.
The matching error selector is defined, confirming the upper-bound guard is real and compilable.

counterargument: One might argue the fix is incomplete because initialize() at line 80-101 also allows setting votingDurationMicros at genesis, and if genesis itself ships a pathological value the guard in setForNextEpoch is moot. However, initialize() (line 92) invokes the same _validateConfig and can only be called by SystemAddresses.GENESIS (line 85) once via the _initialized flag; post-deployment, votingDurationMicros is only writable through setForNextEpoch → applyPendingConfig, both of which are fenced by _validateConfig. A second counter-argument is that now_ + votingDuration could still overflow for extremely distant future timestamps (now_ approaching 2^64 − MAX_VOTING_DURATION). That would require ~584,554 years of elapsed time since Unix epoch in microseconds, which is outside any credible threat model and not what the original issue claimed. Neither counter-argument restores the described exploit.
